narrative: As a white male applicant with an upper-middle-class family background, your law school application stood out in some areas, like your solid undergraduate GPA of 3.30—which is a bit above the typical 3.21 average—and a strong LSAT score of 43, well above the usual 36.70. You're planning to attend full-time, which is the norm for over 90% of students in this dataset. However, the admissions model, trained on past students' profiles to predict first-time bar exam success, estimated a 77.4% chance that you would fail the bar exam, tipping the scales toward rejection to minimize the risk of admitting someone unlikely to pass. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 2, placing you in the bottom 20% of your peers that year, far below the average decile of about 5.5. Next in impact was your Year 1 decile of 2—again, well under the typical 5.7 average—which signaled early academic struggles relative to others and reinforced concerns about your trajectory. Your LSAT score worked strongly in your favor, pulling the prediction toward success and showing your potential in a key area where you outperformed most applicants. Overall, these early and later grade deciles carried the heaviest weight, outweighing your strengths and making your profile riskier compared to typical admitted students who tend to rank higher in school deciles. While your background as a full-time student from a stable family didn't sway things much either way, the model saw your lower deciles as the biggest red flags against bar passage. To move forward, consider retaking courses or programs to boost your decile rankings, as even small improvements there could shift future predictions dramatically. You might also seek targeted bar prep resources early, given your strong LSAT foundation. Reapplying after addressing these academic gaps, perhaps with additional undergrad-level achievements, would demonstrate growth and could align you better with successful profiles. Keep in mind, this is one model's view—many factors beyond predictions influence real admissions.
